moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Grob Opening, an unconventional but surprisingly well-grounded move that immediately stakes a claim on the kingside. By advancing the g-pawn, we control the h5 and f5 squares, preparing to support a potential fianchettoed bishop and create unusual imbalances on the board. While it looks slow compared to 1.e4 or 1.d4, the Grob has a unique character—we're not contesting central control immediately, but rather planning a flexible setup with moves like Bg2, d4, and c4. This opening requires you to embrace asymmetry and punish opponents who play mechanically.

Black claims the center naturally, playing the most natural response to our offbeat opening. By placing a pawn on e5, your opponent controls d4 and f4, preventing us from easily establishing a strong pawn center and forcing us to adapt our plans around their central presence. This is solid, classical play—while we're allowed our experimental first move, Black is right to play for control of the critical d4 and e4 squares rather than panic about our flank play.

Now we prevent Black's pieces from jumping to g4, which could become annoying for our king, and we prepare to support our kingside expansion with moves like g5 if needed. This quiet move also keeps our position flexible—we haven't committed to a fianchetto yet, and we can still play d4 to challenge the center when ready. The move fits the Grob's philosophy of creating an unusual, methodical setup where we're comfortable playing a slightly slower game with spatial advantages on the kingside.

Black develops naturally toward the center, supporting the e5 pawn and preparing to move the knight toward active squares like d4 or e6. This is the London Defense approach—Black isn't overreacting to our flank moves but instead playing solid, principled development while keeping all options open. By controlling d4 and e4 with both pawns and pieces, Black has already established a decent foothold in the center and is ready to challenge us if we ever try to plant a pawn there.
